questions.question: What is poverty?
questions.answer: Poverty is a situation where people are unable to meet their basic needs such as food, shelter, healthcare, education, clean water, and sanitation, often accompanied by a sense of helplessness.
questions.solution: Poverty is multidimensional. It includes hunger, lack of shelter, inability to afford treatment for sick family members, lack of clean water and sanitation, irregular employment, and living with a sense of helplessness. The cases of Ram Saran (urban) and Lakha Singh (rural) illustrate these dimensions.

questions.question: What is the poverty line?
questions.answer: The poverty line is the minimum level of income or consumption necessary to fulfill basic needs. It varies with time and place based on a country's level of development.
questions.solution: A person is considered poor if their income or consumption falls below this minimum level. In India, traditional poverty line estimation considered calorie requirements—2400 calories per day in rural areas and 2100 in urban areas—converted into monetary expenditure. Different countries use different poverty lines appropriate to their development levels.

questions.question: What is the difference between the traditional poverty line and the Multidimensional Poverty Index?
questions.answer: The traditional poverty line is based only on income or consumption expenditure, while the Multidimensional Poverty Index measures poverty through multiple deprivations in health, education, and standard of living.
questions.solution: The traditional poverty line focuses on minimum income/consumption for basic needs. The National Multidimensional Poverty Index (NMPI) uses 12 indicators across three dimensions—health (nutrition, mortality, maternal health), education (schooling, attendance), and standard of living (cooking fuel, sanitation, water, housing, electricity, assets, bank account). A person deprived in any indicator is considered multidimensionally poor.

questions.question: Define social exclusion and vulnerability in the context of poverty.
questions.answer: Social exclusion is the process where individuals or groups are denied equal opportunities and facilities. Vulnerability describes the greater probability of certain groups becoming or remaining poor.
questions.solution: Social exclusion means the poor are forced to live only in poor surroundings, excluded from social equality enjoyed by better-off people. The caste system in India is a typical example. Vulnerability measures the higher risks faced by certain communities (like backward castes) or individuals (like widows) of becoming or remaining poor, especially during natural disasters or economic downturns.

questions.question: What are the calorie requirements used to determine the poverty line in India?
questions.answer: The accepted average calorie requirement is 2400 calories per person per day in rural areas and 2100 calories per person per day in urban areas.
questions.solution: Rural areas have higher calorie requirements because people engage in more physical work than their urban counterparts. These calorie needs were converted into monetary expenditure on food items like cereals, pulses, vegetables, milk, oil, and sugar to determine the poverty line.

questions.question: What is the Head Count Ratio (HCR)?
questions.answer: Head Count Ratio is the proportion of people below the poverty line in a given population, used to estimate the number and percentage of poor people.
questions.solution: HCR helps measure poverty incidence. For example, in 2011-12, India's consumption-based HCR was 22% (26% rural, 14% urban), meaning 22 out of every 100 Indians were below the poverty line. For multidimensional poverty, the HCR was 15% in 2019-21.

questions.question: Name any four vulnerable groups that have higher poverty incidence in India.
questions.answer: Scheduled Tribes, Scheduled Castes, rural agricultural labour households, and urban casual labour households.
questions.solution: As per data, 43% of Scheduled Tribes are poor, followed by 34% of rural agricultural labourers and urban casual labourers, and 29% of Scheduled Castes. The double disadvantage of being a landless casual wage labour household from SC or ST background highlights the severity of poverty among these groups.

questions.question: What is the global poverty line used by international organizations like the World Bank?
questions.answer: International organizations use a uniform standard of $2.15 per person per day (2017 PPP) to measure extreme poverty across countries.
questions.solution: The World Bank uses this standard to make comparisons between countries. The proportion of people living on less than $2.15 per day globally fell from 16.27% in 2010 to 9.05% in 2019. Different countries have different poverty lines based on their national circumstances.

questions.question: What is MGNREGA and when was it enacted?
questions.answer: Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act (MGNREGA), enacted in 2005, provides 100 days of wage employment per household to ensure livelihood security in rural areas.
questions.solution: MGNREGA aims to create sustainable livelihoods and address issues like drought, deforestation, and soil erosion. One-third of the jobs are reserved for women. The wage rate for unskilled manual workers is revised regularly.

questions.question: What are the twin purposes of the Pradhan Mantri Poshan Shakti Abhiyan (PM Poshan)?
questions.answer: PM Poshan aims to (i) improve nutritional requirements, especially calorie and protein intake of children, and (ii) improve school enrolment and eliminate dropouts.
questions.solution: The scheme targets students from Classes I to VIII in government and government-aided schools. It encourages poor children from disadvantaged sections to attend school regularly and helps them concentrate on classroom activities by providing nutritious meals.

questions.question: Compare the two typical cases of poverty—Ram Saran (urban) and Lakha Singh (rural). What dimensions of poverty do they illustrate?
questions.answer: Both cases illustrate landlessness, irregular employment, large family size, low literacy, poor health/nutrition, and helplessness. Ram Saran represents urban poverty with housing insecurity, while Lakha Singh represents rural poverty with lack of healthcare access.
questions.solution: Ram Saran's case shows urban poverty—irregular daily wage work, one-room rented shack, children working to supplement income, meagre meals. Lakha Singh's case shows rural poverty—landlessness, payment in kind, kuchha hut, lack of healthcare (parents died/suffer from TB without treatment). Both illustrate multiple dimensions: unemployment, large families, low literacy levels, poor health, and helplessness.

questions.question: Why did the number of poor remain at about 407 million between 1993-94 and 2004-05 despite the decline in poverty ratio?
questions.answer: The poverty ratio declined from 45% to 37%, but the number of poor remained around 407 million because the total population grew during this period.
questions.solution: When population grows, even if the proportion of poor declines, the absolute number of poor may remain the same or even increase if the decline is not substantial enough to offset population growth. This highlights that poverty reduction in terms of numbers requires both lower proportion and effective population management.

questions.question: Explain how different states in India have adopted different approaches to reduce poverty.
questions.answer: Kerala focused on human resource development, West Bengal implemented land reform measures, while Andhra Pradesh and Tamil Nadu strengthened public distribution of food grains.
questions.solution: Kerala's success in poverty reduction is attributed to investment in education, health, and social indicators. West Bengal's land reforms redistributed assets to rural poor. Andhra Pradesh and Tamil Nadu improved food security through effective PDS. This shows that poverty reduction strategies must be tailored to state-specific conditions and strengths.

questions.question: What is the relationship between economic growth and poverty reduction in India?
questions.answer: Economic growth has a strong link with poverty reduction as it widens opportunities, provides resources for human development, and encourages investment in education for better economic returns.
questions.solution: India's growth rate increased from 3.5% in the 1970s to 6% in the 1980s-90s, with rapid growth continuing in the 21st century. Higher growth created more jobs and incomes, contributing to poverty reduction. However, growth alone is insufficient—targeted anti-poverty programmes are also needed because the poor may not always benefit equally from growth opportunities.

questions.question: Describe the global poverty trends since 2005. Which regions have shown improvement and which remain areas of concern?
questions.answer: Global poverty declined from 16.27% in 2010 to 9.05% in 2019. China and Southeast Asia showed substantial improvement; South Asia also declined. Sub-Saharan Africa remains the region with highest poverty concentration at 35%.
questions.solution: China achieved 0.1% poverty by 2020 due to rapid growth and human capital investment. South Asia's poverty declined from 13% to 11% (2017-2021). However, Sub-Saharan Africa still has 35% poverty, and Latin America saw a slight increase from 4.4% to 4.6%. This shows uneven progress in global poverty reduction.

questions.question: Why are Scheduled Tribes and Scheduled Castes more vulnerable to poverty? Explain.
questions.answer: SC and ST communities face historical discrimination, social exclusion, limited access to education and healthcare, and lack of assets. Their double disadvantage of social backwardness and economic deprivation makes them more vulnerable.
questions.solution: Data shows 43% of ST and 29% of SC are poor—much higher than the national average. The caste system historically denied them equal opportunities in education, employment, and land ownership. This social exclusion combined with economic marginalization (landlessness, low-paying work) creates a vicious cycle of poverty that persists across generations.

questions.question: What is the significance of the Pradhan Mantri Ujjwala Yojana in addressing poverty?
questions.answer: PMUY addresses poverty by providing free LPG connections to poor households, improving health (reducing respiratory disorders), empowering women (connections in their name), and promoting environmental sustainability.
questions.solution: Launched in 2016, PMUY targets BPL, SC, ST, and forest dweller households. By replacing traditional cooking methods (coal, wood, cow dung), it saves women from chronic respiratory diseases, reduces time spent collecting firewood, and lowers deforestation. This multi-dimensional approach addresses health, gender, and environmental aspects of poverty.

questions.question: What are the main causes of poverty in India? Explain any four.
questions.answer: Causes include: (1) colonial economic exploitation, (2) unequal land distribution and ineffective land reforms, (3) limited job creation in industries, (4) high population growth, and (5) indebtedness among small farmers.
questions.solution: Colonial policies ruined traditional industries, creating widespread unemployment. Unequal land distribution left many landless. Green Revolution benefits were limited to select regions. Industries couldn't absorb all job seekers, forcing many into informal sector with irregular income. High population growth diluted development gains. Small farmers borrow at high risk, leading to debt traps that perpetuate poverty.

questions.question: What is the difference between consumption-based poverty estimates and multidimensional poverty estimates?
questions.answer: Consumption-based estimates measure poverty solely by income or expenditure levels. Multidimensional estimates measure poverty through multiple deprivations in health, education, and standard of living using 12 indicators.
questions.solution: Consumption-based estimates focus only on monetary inadequacy. Multidimensional estimates recognize that poverty is more than low income—it includes lack of nutrition, education, sanitation, clean water, housing, and assets. The NMPI complements income-based measures by capturing these deprivations directly, providing a more complete picture of poverty.

questions.question: How does the Pradhan Mantri Surakshit Matritva Abhiyan help in poverty reduction?
questions.answer: PMSMA reduces maternal and infant mortality by providing quality antenatal care to pregnant women, preventing health crises that can push families deeper into poverty due to medical expenses.
questions.solution: Launched in 2016, PMSMA provides services on the ninth day of every month, engaging private practitioners as volunteers. By ensuring safe pregnancies and deliveries, it reduces the financial burden of maternal healthcare on poor families. Healthy mothers and children contribute to breaking the intergenerational cycle of poverty.

questions.question: Analyze why poverty reduction has been faster in some states like Kerala and Tamil Nadu compared to others like Bihar and Uttar Pradesh.
questions.answer: Kerala and Tamil Nadu invested heavily in human resource development, education, and health infrastructure, while also implementing effective PDS and land reforms. Bihar and UP lagged in these areas despite recent improvements.
questions.solution: Kerala's focus on universal education, healthcare, and social indicators created a skilled, healthy workforce that could access better opportunities. Tamil Nadu's effective PDS and industrial growth contributed. In contrast, Bihar and UP had lower literacy rates, poor health infrastructure, and ineffective land reforms, keeping poverty higher. However, recent years show these states making remarkable strides in poverty reduction.

questions.question: Explain the concepts of social exclusion and vulnerability with examples. How do they help in understanding poverty beyond income measures?
questions.answer: Social exclusion denies equal opportunities to groups (e.g., caste-based discrimination), while vulnerability measures higher risk of poverty (e.g., widows, disabled). They reveal poverty as a social process, not just income deficiency.
questions.solution: Social exclusion—the caste system excludes certain groups from education, jobs, and social equality, creating poverty that persists across generations. Vulnerability—a widow or physically handicapped person has fewer options for earning livelihood and faces greater risks during crises. These concepts show poverty is about relationships, power, and risk, not just money. They help design targeted interventions for excluded and vulnerable groups.

questions.question: How has India's progress in poverty reduction contributed to achieving Sustainable Development Goal 1? What challenges remain?
questions.answer: India reduced multidimensional poverty from 25% in 2015-16 to 15% in 2019-21, with 13.5 crore escaping poverty, contributing to SDG 1 (No Poverty). Challenges include inter-state disparities, vulnerable groups still facing high poverty, and broadening poverty definition to human poverty.
questions.solution: This progress reflects India's commitment to SDG 1's target of halving poverty. However, challenges remain: wide rural-urban and inter-state disparities persist; certain groups like ST (43% poor) still have high poverty; and the concept must broaden from income poverty to human poverty—considering whether people have education, shelter, healthcare, job security, and freedom from discrimination.

questions.question: Evaluate the effectiveness of MGNREGA as an anti-poverty programme. What are its strengths and limitations?
questions.answer: MGNREGA provides livelihood security through 100 days guaranteed employment, empowers women with one-third reservation, and addresses sustainable development. Limitations include implementation challenges and seasonal nature of work.
questions.solution: Strengths: Legal guarantee of employment, creates durable assets (water conservation, soil improvement), reduces distress migration, empowers women. Limitations: Coverage limited to 100 days, wage rates sometimes below minimum, implementation varies across states, work availability often seasonal. Despite limitations, it has significantly improved rural livelihoods and reduced poverty vulnerability.

questions.question: What do you understand by human poverty? How is it different from the traditional concept of poverty?
questions.answer: Human poverty goes beyond income to include lack of education, shelter, healthcare, job security, self-confidence, and freedom from discrimination. Traditional poverty focuses only on minimum subsistence level.
questions.solution: Income-based poverty measures only a 'minimum' subsistence level—whether people can afford basic food and necessities. Human poverty asks broader questions: Do people have education? Quality healthcare? Decent housing? Job security? Self-confidence? Are they free from caste/gender discrimination? Is child labour common? Worldwide experience shows that as development progresses, the definition of poverty evolves from mere survival to quality of life and dignity.

questions.question: How do the 12 indicators of the National Multidimensional Poverty Index capture different dimensions of poverty? Explain with examples.
questions.answer: The 12 indicators cover health (nutrition, mortality, maternal health), education (schooling, attendance), and standard of living (cooking fuel, sanitation, water, housing, electricity, assets, bank account), providing a comprehensive picture of deprivations.
questions.solution: Health indicators: Nutrition captures undernourishment; mortality captures child/adolescent deaths; maternal health captures access to skilled care during childbirth. Education: Years of schooling shows overall educational attainment; school attendance shows current enrollment. Standard of living: Cooking fuel (clean vs biomass), sanitation (improved facilities), water (access), housing (quality materials), electricity, assets (ownership), bank account (financial inclusion). These 12 indicators together reveal whether a person experiences multiple simultaneous deprivations.

questions.question: Explain the link between poverty, indebtedness, and social obligations among poor families in India.
questions.answer: Poor families spend heavily on social obligations and religious ceremonies, often borrowing at high interest rates. Unable to repay, they fall into debt traps, perpetuating poverty.
questions.solution: Small farmers need credit for agricultural inputs but lack savings. They borrow from informal sources at high interest. Additionally, social and religious obligations force expenditure beyond their means. Default leads to loss of assets and deeper poverty. Indebtedness becomes both a cause (borrowing leads to debt) and effect (poverty forces borrowing) of poverty, creating a vicious cycle that is difficult to break.

questions.question: Why is it important to understand poverty as a multidimensional concept rather than just income deficiency? Discuss with reference to the cases of Ram Saran and Lakha Singh.
questions.answer: The cases show poverty includes landlessness, unemployment, large families, illiteracy, poor health, and helplessness—not just low income. A purely income-based view would miss these interconnected deprivations.
questions.solution: Ram Saran's family has low income, but also: no land, irregular employment, 6 family members, children working instead of studying, limited healthcare, poor housing, sense of helplessness. Lakha Singh's family similarly faces landlessness, erratic income, untreated TB, no education for adults, lack of basic amenities. Both cases demonstrate that poverty is a web of interconnected deprivations—income is just one part. Addressing poverty requires tackling all these dimensions simultaneously.
